text: The surgeon performs a transabdominal preperitoneal robotic repair, entering the abdomen to find the omentum incarcerated within the hernia defect. The omentum is gradually reduced over fifteen to twenty minutes. Following reduction, the surgeon begins pre-pectineal dissection and divides the falciform ligament.
The surgeon successfully reduces the hernia sac in its entirety, removing the contents from the hernia defect.

The surgeon performs a circumferential pre-peritoneal dissection to facilitate mesh placement, encountering a more adherent peritoneum to the diaphragm compared to typical ventral hernias. A large amount of fat is observed and reduced from the left side of the hernia, necessitating a larger dissection due to bilateral defects.
The surgeon performs a tedious preperitoneal dissection to prepare for mesh placement. Once sufficient dissection is achieved, the hernia defects are closed using a running absorbable OV-lock suture.

The video shows the final closure of the hernia defect with a barred soft preperitoneal macroporous polypropylene mesh in place. The surgeon performs a peritoneal closure to exclude the mesh from the peritoneal cavity.
